source_metadata.title_raw: Kasey Castillo - I-18-238 - November 6, 2018 - Canyon Lake

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 6, 2018

Kasey Castillo
22616 Canyon Club Dr.
Canyon Lake, CA 92587

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. 1-18-238

Dear Ms. Castillo:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Because your inquiry is general in nature and does not pertain to a specific governmental decision, we are treating it as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

If elected to the Canyon Lake City Council, would the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from making any governmental decisions given the facts you have provided?

CONCLUSION

Absent facts regarding a specific governmental decision, we can only advise that if a decision materially affects your business, or clients of your business, you will be required to disqualify yourself from participating in the decision. This determination is made on a case-by-case basis according to the facts pertaining to the particular matter. Thus, without the factual context of a specific decision, we can provide general information but are unable to provide specific advice.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You currently co-own a law firm in Riverside and are considering running for a seat on the Canyon Lake City Council. A portion of your law practice is the representation of first responders. Your firm acts as general counsel to certain police and fire associations statewide. Your practice

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. 1-18-238
Page No. 2

deals with these associations and individuals in the areas of employment law and criminal defense,
while also representing them in civil litigation, or in family law matters.

The City of Canyon Lake currently contracts with the County of Riverside for police
(Riverside Sheriff) and fire (Cal Fire) services. You have never represented or consulted for the
Sheriff's Department or its employees in contract negotiations of any type. A member of your firm
has previously represented two individuals in disciplinary appeals from termination as Sheriff's
deputies by the county of Riverside; their appeals came after they were no longer employed, and
were not paid for by any employee association. Disciplinary matters are handled at the county level
(not by the contract city), and they did not involve deputies assigned to service the City of Canyon
Lake. Both cases have resolved. Neither you nor your law firm have any financial agreements with
the County, or the Sheriff's Department, nor do you anticipate any.

A Riverside Sheriff's employee recently retained the law firm to prosecute a civil lawsuit
against the Riverside Sheriff's Department for First Amendment retaliation claims. As such, your
law firm would be prosecuting the individual employee against the County of Riverside, the
current Sheriff, and some Sheriff's employees. To your knowledge, the defendants in the lawsuit
are not assigned to the City of Canyon Lake (the contract City). The lawsuit has no connection to
the City of Canyon Lake, contracts with the City of Canyon Lake, or employees of the City of
Canyon Lake.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, participating
in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that would have
a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests
distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.)

An official's financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest
under the Act are set forth in Section 87103 and include all of the following:

• Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or
more (subdivision (a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management (subdivision (d)).

• Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more.
(Subdivision (b).)

• Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value to the official in the 12 months
prior to when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (c).)

• Any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating $470 or more to the official in the 12 months prior
to when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (e); Regulation 18940.2.)

• An interest in the official's personal finances or those of immediate family members.

File No. I-18-238
Page No. 3

Based on the facts provided, you would have an interest in your law practice as a business entity and source of income, an interest in any clients of the business from whom you have received income aggregating $500 or more in the previous 12 months as sources of income, and an interest in your personal finances.

The Act does not prohibit you from serving on the City Council while simultaneously practicing law in a private capacity. However, to the extent that any decision before you in your governmental capacity may have a financial effect on your financial interests including your law firm and clients of your firm, you may be prohibited from taking part in the decision.

Please note that the determination of a conflict of interest is dependent upon the facts involved in a particular decision. We recommend you contact our office in the event that you are concerned about a particular decision if you are elected. In addition, you can consult our website for further information concerning conflicts of interest under the Act, including the publication titled "Recognizing Conflicts of Interest."

Also, please be aware that Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, from participating in the making of contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. Please contact us for additional advice in the event that a decision involves a contract in which you have a financial interest such as any contract between the City and your firm or clients of your firm.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

³ See http://www.fppc.ca.gov/content/dam/fppc/NS-Documents/LegalDiv/Conflicts%20of%20Interest/Conflicts-Guide-August-2015-Jan-2016-Edits.pdf.
